original_hypothesis.title: Auction address can be set to non-auction contract, enabling malicious conversion math to misallocate deposits (economic manipulation)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: GenesisPool.setAuction (onlyManagerOrProtocol) accepts any non-zero address, with no verification against AuctionFactory.isAuction(). A malicious auction can return manipulated getNativeTokenAmount/getFundingTokenAmount causing deposits to be capped incorrectly, allocatedNativeAmount to be wrong, or eligibility thresholds to be met spuriously, impacting the launch ratio and depositor fairness.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Even though access is privileged, it is a concrete code gap: the pool trusts auction math for critical accounting and threshold logic. Misconfiguration or malicious setting can lead to user harm.
deployment_assumptions: Manager or tokenOwner (per onlyManagerOrProtocol) sets auction to a contract not vetted by AuctionFactory
Users deposit based on expected pricing/ratio derived from auction
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No code-level enforcement that auction is registered in AuctionFactory; GenesisPool.setAuction only checks _auction != address(0).

proof_of_signal.lines: setAuction(): require(_auction != address(0)) and status check only; no factory/isAuction validation
proof_of_signal.pattern: Unvalidated external dependency for core accounting

preconditions: Deposits/eligibility logic depends on auction conversion outputs

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External oracle/math contract address not validated
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Malicious auction can skew allocations/threshold checks to favor token owner or deny depositors fair ratios

analysis_questions: Is AuctionFactory intended as an allowlist? If yes, why isn't it enforced in GenesisPool?
Can an attacker profit by setting auction to over-credit nativeAmount to reach eligibility early and then launch with bad ratios?
Are UIs/users relying on FixedAuction-style predictable math?
validation_requirements: Construct a malicious auction that returns extreme ratios; simulate depositToken allocations and eligibility transitions
Check whether GenesisPoolManager.setAuction (Governance) uses allowlisting or can be abused similarly

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Token owner sets auction to return inflated nativeAmount per funding deposit, causing allocatedNativeAmount to hit proposedNativeAmount early with minimal deposits, then launches and captures disproportionate LP share vs depositors.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Depositors may receive materially worse value; launch economics can be manipulated
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All depositors in affected genesis pools
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult after launch; may require governance remediation

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests ensuring auction addresses are allowlisted/vetted

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare on-chain auction address to AuctionFactory registry

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
